primary_diagnosis.name: Acute urinary retention, likely secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia precipitated by decongestant use

comorbidities.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (presumed, based on 6-month symptom history)

pathophysiology_summary: A 6-month history of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms (hesitancy, poor stream, nocturia) suggests underlying bladder outlet obstruction from benign prostatic hyperplasia. The recent addition of a decongestant (an alpha-adrenergic agonist that increases urethral sphincter tone) has precipitated complete decompensation into acute urinary retention. The massively distended, painful bladder places the patient at risk of post-renal acute kidney injury from back-pressure on the kidneys, and prolonged retention can also cause bladder wall damage. Overflow incontinence (the reported dribbling) reflects pressure exceeding sphincter resistance rather than voluntary voiding. This is a urological emergency requiring prompt catheter decompression, which cannot be performed via telehealth chat.

differential.evidence_basis: Complete inability to void with severe suprapubic pain.
Can cause urinary symptoms and suprapubic discomfort, but complete inability to void with a palpable mass is not typical of simple cystitis.
Can cause lower abdominal discomfort and contribute to outlet obstruction; should be screened for alongside the urinary picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Complete inability to void with severe suprapubic pain should place acute retention as leading from the opening complaint.

differential.diagnosis: Acute urinary retention secondary to BPH, precipitated by decongestant
differential.evidence_basis: Progressive LUTS history (DI-002) plus recent decongestant use (DI-003) together identify both the underlying cause and the acute precipitant.
Absence of fever and the presence of a massive, painful, palpable bladder are inconsistent with simple cystitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the diagnosis and precipitant should be clear, and the priority is urgent catheter decompression.

differential.diagnosis: Acute urinary retention, likely secondary to BPH precipitated by decongestant use
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; requires urgent in-person catheterisation, unavailable via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan without urgent same-day in-person catheterisation is inadequate given the risk of renal impairment.

red_flags.description: Complete inability to void for 14 hours with a constant urge and severe suprapubic pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates a massively distended bladder at risk of post-renal AKI and requiring urgent decompression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prolonged retention progresses to acute kidney injury and worsening bladder wall damage.

red_flags.description: Palpable, dull, tender suprapubic mass extending to the umbilicus (clinician-only finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms the severity of bladder distension; requires abdominal examination unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — reliance on the historical severity and duration substitutes for this.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Suggesting home remedies for bladder decompression
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might suggest warm baths, running water sounds, or other self-help voiding techniques as a first step — these are inappropriate given the severity and duration of complete retention, which requires prompt catheterisation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Digital rectal examination of the prostate
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person medical review (GP urgent appointment, urgent care clinic, or ED) for catheter decompression
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Any delay in arranging catheterisation risks progression to post-renal acute kidney injury and worsening pain.
